best_void.paper_b_title: Peephole: Predicting Network Performance Before Training

interpretability_audit.a_represents: embedded/architecture community focused on designing high-throughput multicore processor hardware for neural-network streaming inference
interpretability_audit.b_represents: AutoML/NAS and performance-modeling community focused on predicting end-to-end network performance (latency/throughput) prior to training
interpretability_audit.reason: Ansor’s landmark bridge is specifically between DNN operator/tensor-program optimization in ML frameworks and classical compiler autotuning (loop transformations, tiling, code generation) using an ML-guided cost model over a compiler search space. Paper A (embedded multicore NN processors) aligns more with hardware/architecture for NN execution than with compiler-level tensor program generation, and Paper B (predicting network performance before training) is closer to NAS/system-level performance prediction than to operator-level schedule search and compiler optimization. While both touch “performance for deep learning,” they do not cleanly correspond to the two communities Ansor directly bridged (tensor-operator compiler autotuning ↔ compiler optimization/codegen), so this A/B pair is not a strong pre-2020 bridge hypothesis for Ansor.

best_void.paper_b_title: Towards a Grid-based approach to Traffic Routing in VANET

interpretability_audit.a_represents: GPU architecture/performance/energy-efficiency analysis community (green computing, GPU power/energy optimization, systems evaluation)
interpretability_audit.b_represents: vehicular ad hoc networking (VANET) and traffic-routing algorithms community (network protocols, routing in mobile networks)
interpretability_audit.reason: FlashAttention’s bridge is specifically between Transformer attention algorithm design and hardware memory hierarchy / IO-complexity thinking (SRAM vs HBM, bandwidth-aware tiling). Source A is hardware/GPU energy-efficiency surveying, which is at best a distant cousin of hardware performance analysis and does not naturally connect to exact attention computation or IO-optimal attention kernels as a two-community bridge. Source B is about VANET traffic routing, a networking domain that does not map onto either Transformer attention algorithms or memory/IO complexity. Together, A and B do not plausibly represent the two communities that FlashAttention later bridged.

best_void.paper_b_title: Towards a Grid-based approach to Traffic Routing in VANET

interpretability_audit.a_represents: GPU power/energy efficiency research and survey community (GPU architecture, energy optimization techniques, performance-per-watt analysis)
interpretability_audit.b_represents: Vehicular ad-hoc networking (VANET) and traffic routing/ITS networking community (routing protocols, grid-based routing in mobile networks)
interpretability_audit.reason: The landmark paper bridges LLM inference serving (especially KV-cache allocation/fragmentation) with OS virtual memory ideas (paging, demand-driven allocation). Source A is about GPU energy efficiency methods, which relates to hardware power optimization rather than OS-like paging abstractions for dynamic memory management in serving systems. Source B is about network-layer routing in VANETs, which is orthogonal to memory management and LLM serving. Together, A and B do not correspond to the two communities (LLM serving ↔ OS VM/demand paging) that PagedAttention connects, so they do not form a plausible pre-publication bridge hypothesis.

landmark.b_side: ML hardware accelerators / domain-specific languages (XLA, TPU, custom ASIC)
landmark.bridge_rationale: Unified multi-level IR hierarchy allowing compiler and ML hardware designers to share infrastructure

interpretability_audit.a_represents: Pattern recognition / early AI theory applied to management or decision-support systems
interpretability_audit.b_represents: IP/MPLS networking / traffic engineering and network operations research
interpretability_audit.reason: The MLIR landmark bridges compiler infrastructure (LLVM-style IRs, optimization pipelines, code generation) with ML hardware accelerators and domain-specific ML compiler stacks (e.g., XLA/TPU, DSLs). Source A (pattern recognition theory for “intellectual network management facilities”) and Source B (traffic engineering in IP/MPLS networks) instead represent AI/pattern-recognition and networking/telecom operations communities, respectively. Neither naturally maps onto the LLVM/IR/compiler-toolchain ecosystem nor the ML-accelerator/DSL compiler community that MLIR unified, so this A/B pair is not a plausible pre-publication bridge hypothesis for MLIR.

best_void.paper_b_title: A Survey on Deep Packet Inspection for Intrusion Detection Systems

interpretability_audit.a_represents: Kernel-method / multiple-kernel learning research in machine learning (classical representation + similarity learning, pre-deep/GNN era)
interpretability_audit.b_represents: Network security / intrusion detection community focused on deep packet inspection (traffic/content-based IDS, often ML/DL for network data)
interpretability_audit.reason: Devign’s landmark bridge is specifically between software vulnerability detection/static analysis over program structure and graph neural networks/program representation learning. Paper B is in security, but it is network intrusion detection via deep packet inspection rather than program analysis or code vulnerability identification, so it only weakly overlaps at the high-level “security detection” theme. Paper A (deep multiple kernel learning) aligns with older kernel-based ML methodology rather than graph-based program semantics or GNN-style representation learning that Devign leverages. Together, A and B do not cleanly map onto the two communities Devign later bridged, nor do they suggest the key CFG/DFG/AST-to-GNN program-graph framing.
